original_hypothesis.title: lzCompose Permanently Reverts via payable.transfer in _refundToken
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: _refundToken uses payable(_refundAddress).transfer(_nativeAmount) which forwards only 2300 gas. If _cantoRefundAddress is a smart contract wallet (Gnosis Safe, AA wallet) or a contract without a receive function, this reverts. Since _refundToken is called from every failure path in lzCompose, and lzCompose MUST NOT revert per LayerZero spec, any revert permanently locks the OFT tokens delivered by LayerZero in the router.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The documented design intent states lzCompose must never revert. payable.transfer is known to fail with many contract recipients. This creates a permanent fund lock for any user whose refund address is a contract.

proof_of_signal.pattern: payable(_refundAddress).transfer(_nativeAmount) — 2300 gas stipend reverts for contract recipients; uncaught revert propagates to lzCompose

preconditions: _cantoRefundAddress or composeFrom is a contract without simple receive/fallback

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Gnosis Safe, Account Abstraction wallets, and many contract wallets need >2300 gas to receive ETH
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should use (bool success,) = _refundAddress.call{value: _nativeAmount}('') with failure handling

analysis_questions: What percentage of users use smart contract wallets on Canto?
Can the LZ executor retry a failed lzCompose, or are tokens permanently locked?
Is there any recovery mechanism for tokens stuck in the router?
validation_requirements: Test lzCompose failure path with Gnosis Safe as _cantoRefundAddress
Confirm entire lzCompose reverts when payable.transfer fails
Verify LayerZero message retry behavior when lzCompose reverts

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Legitimate user with a multisig or smart contract wallet sets it as refund address. Any failure in the pipeline causes payable.transfer to revert, reverting lzCompose, permanently locking the user's OFT tokens in the router.
prerequisites: Refund address is a contract that consumes >2300 gas on receive

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent loss of user's cross-chain transfer amount
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any user with a contract wallet as refund address
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None without contract upgrade - tokens locked permanently

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test with contract wallet as refund address

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: 2300 gas stipend vs common wallet receive costs
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as failed lzCompose tx on-chain

validation_result.mechanism: payable.transfer 2300 gas DoS in non-reverting compose callback
validation_result.impact_summary: If any failure path in lzCompose triggers _refundToken with msg.value > 0 and the refund address is a contract requiring >2300 gas to receive ETH, the entire lzCompose reverts, permanently locking the user's OFT tokens in the router.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The _refundToken function at asdRouter.sol uses payable(_refundAddress).transfer(_nativeAmount) to refund native tokens. Solidity's .transfer() forwards only 2300 gas to the recipient. Smart contract wallets (Gnosis Safe, ERC-4337 Account Abstraction wallets, and many other contract wallets) require significantly more than 2300 gas in their receive/fallback functions. When .transfer() is called to such a contract, it reverts. Since _refundToken is an internal function called directly from lzCompose without any try/catch wrapping, the revert propagates up to lzCompose itself. The code comment explicitly states lzCompose 'Cannot revert anywhere, must send the tokens to the intended receiver if something fails (token's will be lost otherwise)'. Despite this design intent, the payable.transfer creates a revert path. Once lzCompose reverts, the OFT tokens already delivered by LayerZero to the router contract remain locked with no recovery mechanism, as the compose message cannot be replayed successfully (it will revert again on retry).
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal is accurate. The _refundToken function uses payable(_refundAddress).transfer(_nativeAmount) which forwards only 2300 gas. This is called from every failure path in lzCompose where msg.value is passed as _nativeAmount. When msg.value > 0, the transfer is attempted. If _refundAddress is a contract that needs >2300 gas on receive, the transfer reverts, and the revert propagates to lzCompose since there is no try/catch or low-level call wrapping.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: payable(_refundAddress).transfer(_nativeAmount) forwards only 2300 gas. If _refundAddress is a contract requiring >2300 gas to receive ETH (e.g., Gnosis Safe, AA wallet), this reverts. The revert propagates to lzCompose since there is no try/catch.

Every failure path calls _refundToken with msg.value as _nativeAmount. If msg.value > 0, the payable.transfer in _refundToken can revert, causing the entire lzCompose to revert and permanently locking delivered OFT tokens.

calling_sequence: 1. LayerZero executor calls lzCompose with msg.value > 0 (to cover destination gas fees)
2. Any failure condition triggers (e.g., non-whitelisted token, swap failure, deposit failure, insufficient fee)
6. If _refundAddress is a contract needing >2300 gas on receive, transfer reverts
7. Revert propagates through _refundToken (internal, no try/catch) to lzCompose
8. lzCompose reverts, violating the non-revert requirement
9. OFT tokens already delivered to router are permanently locked
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: lzCompose -> (failure condition) -> _refundToken -> payable(_refundAddress).transfer(_nativeAmount) -> REVERT (if contract recipient with >2300 gas receive). The revert propagates up since _refundToken is an internal function with no error handling around the .transfer() call. The lzCompose function itself has no top-level try/catch.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment configuration affects this vulnerability. The deployASDRouter.js script deploys the contract as-is. The payable.transfer pattern is baked into the contract code.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation:
Scenario 1 (Legitimate user): A user with a Gnosis Safe multisig or Account Abstraction wallet on Canto sets it as their _cantoRefundAddress. They initiate a cross-chain OFT transfer. If any step fails (swap slippage, non-whitelisted token after a config change, etc.), _refundToken is called. The payable.transfer to their multisig fails because Gnosis Safe's fallback requires >2300 gas. lzCompose reverts, and their OFT tokens are permanently locked in the router.

Scenario 2 (Intentional griefing): An attacker deploys a contract with a receive() function that always reverts or consumes >2300 gas. They set this as _cantoRefundAddress and send a cross-chain transfer designed to fail (e.g., with _minAmountASD set impossibly high). When the swap fails and _refundToken is called, the payable.transfer reverts, causing lzCompose to revert permanently for that message.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL. Any user whose refund address is a contract wallet (Gnosis Safe, ERC-4337 wallets, or any contract with non-trivial receive logic) risks permanent loss of their cross-chain transfer amount if any failure condition is triggered in lzCompose. This is not a theoretical concern - smart contract wallets are increasingly common, especially among institutional users and DAOs. On Canto, where the protocol operates, even EIP-3074 or future account abstraction adoption would expand the affected user base. There is no recovery mechanism in the contract for locked tokens.
recommended_mitigations: Replace payable(_refundAddress).transfer(_nativeAmount) with a low-level call: (bool success, ) = _refundAddress.call{value: _nativeAmount}(""); If the call fails, store the refund in a mapping for later claim by the recipient (pull pattern).

Wrap the entire native token refund in a try/catch-equivalent pattern: attempt the call, and if it fails, wrap the ETH as WETH and transfer the WETH token instead (WETH transfer won't fail for contract recipients).
Also replace IERC20(_tokenAddress).transfer(_refundAddress, _amount) with SafeERC20.safeTransfer wrapped in a try/catch to prevent ERC20 transfer failures from reverting lzCompose.
Consider implementing a top-level try/catch in lzCompose that catches any unexpected reverts and stores tokens for manual recovery.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy ASDRouter on a local fork of Canto. 2. Deploy a MockContract that has a receive() function consuming >2300 gas (e.g., writing to storage in receive). 3. Set up a mock LayerZero endpoint and whitelisted OFT token. 4. Call lzCompose with msg.value > 0, a properly formatted composeMsg with _cantoRefundAddress set to the MockContract, and parameters designed to trigger a failure (e.g., non-whitelisted _from address). 5. Observe that lzCompose reverts due to the payable.transfer failure in _refundToken. 6. Confirm OFT tokens remain locked in the router.
uncertainties: The exact behavior of LayerZero v2's executor when lzCompose reverts - whether it retries (which would fail again) or marks the message as failed with potential manual recovery through the endpoint.
Whether LayerZero v2 has a built-in stored message / retry mechanism that could allow eventual recovery of the locked tokens through the endpoint's lzComposeAlert or similar recovery functions.
additional_testing_needed: Test with actual Gnosis Safe proxy as refund address to confirm 2300 gas is insufficient
Test LayerZero v2 executor retry/recovery behavior when lzCompose reverts
Measure gas costs of common contract wallet receive() functions on Canto
Test with ERC-4337 SimpleAccount as refund address
